Functionally, it is mainly used as a control in experiments such as SPR. By comparing its binding with target RNA (e.g., ⅡBh), it evaluates the binding specificity of compounds to ⅡBh and assists in distinguishing non-specific binding from specific interactions.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[3-(2-aminoethyl)-5-[2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-methoxyphenyl]-2-methoxyphenyl]ethanamine (also referred to as the target compound) was synthesized via palladium-catalyzed Suzuki coupling reactions and characterized by methods such as Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) and Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR); functionally, this molecule can bind to the hairpin RNA (Bh) of the HIV-1 RRE subdomain IIB, though a high ligand/Bh molar ratio is required to produce detectable spectral changes and its binding affinity is relatively low, and it is a complex molecule containing multiple primary amine groups (RNH2) and a polyphenol ether structure, whose scaffold is composed of two substituted phenyl rings linked by an ether oxygen, with a total of four 2-aminoethyl side chains and one methoxy group on the rings, and this polyamine structure grants the molecule high cationic character and hydrophilicity at physiological pH, enabling strong interaction with anionic biomolecules, while in practice, polyamine-like molecules are often used as non-viral gene delivery vectors, forming stable complexes with nucleic acids (DNA/RNA) to facilitate transfection, or as modulators of polyamine metabolic pathways, and its high cationic nature also suggests potential antimicrobial or antifungal activity.

RNA information: The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).; The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).·
Small_molecule_information: N,N-diethyl-N-[5-methyl-7-[2-(4-methylmorpholin-4-ium-4-yl)ethoxy]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-yl]ethane-1,2-diamine diiodide is a dicationic benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline analog where the piperidinium is replaced by a morpholinium quaternary ammonium salt. This fused quinoline core is a common scaffold in medicinal chemistry, primarily screened for cytotoxic activity against various cancer cell lines.

RNA information: RNA5 is a 29-nt synthetic hairpin RNA based on SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5, containing a single G bulge. It spontaneously folds into an A-form helix with a flexible bulge site, enabling minor-groove recognition. RNA5 is used for GaMD simulations, NMR R2 mapping, and Kd measurements to probe selective ligand binding.
Small_molecule_information: 3-(7-fluoroimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-2-yl)-7-piperazin-1-ylchromen-2-one, also referred to as C30, is a complex heterocyclic compound comprising a coumarin (chromen-2-one) scaffold, an imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine ring, and a piperazine ring, which is a planar coumarin derivative with a piperazine A-ring and imidazopyridine DE-fused system and bears 1 charge. The coumarin structure (benzopyranone) is the core of many natural products and fluorescent probes, and substituents at the 3 and 7 positions offer high chemical and biological functional diversity. The imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine moiety is introduced to enhance the molecules affinity for biological targets, and the fluorine atom at the 7-position is a common modification in medicinal chemistry to improve metabolic stability and lipophilicity. The 7-piperazin-1-yl group is a basic center often used to modulate drug solubility, permeability, and interaction with targets like GPCRs or ion channels, and this compound binds the single-G bulge of RNA1/RNA5 via minor-groove H-bonds, -stacking and electrostatics, with this binding validated by FP, MST, NMR and GaMD. Given its complex structure, 3-(7-fluoroimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-2-yl)-7-piperazin-1-ylchromen-2-one is likely designed as a potential fluorescent probe, as coumarin structures often fluoresce, and it may also be a highly selective drug candidate explored for treating central nervous system disorders or as a kinase inhibitor, while also serving as lead for selective RNA ligands.

RNA information: The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).; The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).·
Small_molecule_information: 5-(diaminomethylideneamino)-2-[[2-[(5-methyl-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-yl)amino]acetyl]amino]pentanoic acid is the benzofuroquinoline core conjugated via glycine to arginine. The guanidino group of arginine is key to cell penetrating properties and nucleic acid affinity. This structure is a typical cell-penetrating drug precursor used in anticancer and antiviral research to enhance cellular uptake and nuclear targeting capabilities.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-(3,4,5-trihydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one, also referred to as Myricetin, is a natural flavonol compound with the formula C15H10O8, and is a flavonoid with antioxidant, anticancer, and neuroprotective effects that binds to CAG repeat RNA, inhibiting toxic protein aggregation in Huntingtons disease. Its structure is a flavonol backbone with hydroxyl groups on the A ring (5 and 7) and C ring (3), and crucially, three hydroxyl groups on the B ring (3, 4, and 5 positions, a pyrogallol moiety), and its polyphenolic structure enables metal chelation and free radical scavenging. Myricetin is widely found in fruits, vegetables, berries, wine, and tea. Functionally, possessing six hydroxyl groups, particularly the pyrogallol structure on the B ring, Myricetin is recognized as one of the most powerful natural antioxidants known, capable of efficiently scavenging various free radicals and protecting cells from oxidative stress, and it also exhibits significant anti-inflammatory, antiviral, antitumor, and cardiovascular protective effects, often by modulating signaling pathways and enzyme activities, in addition to its anticancer and neuroprotective properties linked to its binding with CAG repeat RNA and inhibition of toxic protein aggregation in Huntingtons disease. Due to its potent antioxidant capacity, it is extensively studied and applied in nutritional supplements and natural drug development.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a short, genetically conserved stem–bulge–loop hairpin found at the 5′ end of every viral transcript.  Functional constructs range from minimal 29-nt fragments (containing the essential bulge and loop) up to the canonical 52–59-nt forms seen in full-length genomes; in all cases the active fold is the same: a double-stranded stem interrupted by a three-base pyrimidine bulge (commonly UCU) and capped by a six-nucleotide loop.  This bulge-and-loop motif first captures an arginine-rich segment of the viral Tat protein, inducing a local conformational switch that allows Tat to recruit the host Cyclin T1–CDK9 (P-TEFb) complex.  The resultant Tat–TAR–P-TEFb assembly converts RNA polymerase II from abortive to highly processive elongation, boosting viral gene expression >100-fold, and the same RNA structure also facilitates strand-transfer during reverse transcription.  Because the bulge–loop signature is indispensable and sequence-invariant across strains, TAR acts as a natural “riboswitch-like” sensor that tunes the balance between transcription and genome replication, offering a structurally small yet functionally critical target for therapeutic intervention.
Small_molecule_information: 2-chloro-10-[3-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)propyl]phenothiazine, also referred to as Prochlorperazine, is a phenothiazine derivative, substituted with a chlorine atom at the 2-position and a 3-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)propyl side chain at the 10-position, and it is also a phenothiazine-class antipsychotic that was repurposed as a small-molecule ligand for the HIV-1 TAR RNA bulge, retaining the tricyclic phenothiazine core that stacks between unpaired purines and pyrimidines of the bulge while its N-ethyl and 2-chloro substituents provide the shape and charge complementarity needed to occupy the Tat-binding pocket; the chlorine atom increases the molecules lipophilicity, and in vitro EMSA shows that this compound disrupts the TatTARcyclin T1 complex, in HeLa reporter assays it suppresses Tat-dependent LTR trans-activation without affecting a heterologous RevRRE control, NMR titrations with uniformly 13C/15N-labeled TAR demonstrate that the drug induces chemical-shift perturbations exclusively in the 5 bulge even at a certain excess, confirming RNA-specific recognition analogous to FMN riboswitch binding, thus exemplifying a non-nucleotide, non-peptide scaffold that can be optimized into selective anti-HIV agents targeting RNA instead of protein; given its strong structural resemblance to clinically used antipsychotics (like Chlorpromazine), this compound is primarily investigated for its powerful antipsychotic effects, it works by blocking dopamine receptors in the brain, offering significant efficacy against schizophrenia and strong antiemetic (anti-vomiting) properties.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 4-amino-1-[(2R,5S)-2-(hydroxymethyl)-1,3-oxathiolan-5-yl]pyrimidin-2-one, also referred to as Lamivudine (3TC) or its enantiomer, is a synthetic cytidine nucleoside analog where the 3 oxygen atom of the ribose ring is replaced by a sulfur atom, forming a 1,3-oxathiolane sugar ring, molecular formula C8H11N3O3S; it is a nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NRTI) that incorporates into viral DNA, terminating its synthesis, and in terms of real-world activity, Lamivudine is an extremely important antiviral drug, classified as a Nucleoside Reverse Transcriptase Inhibitor (NRTI), which is widely used clinically for the treatment of Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV) infection and Hepatitis B Virus (HBV) infection, with its mechanism of action involving being phosphorylated intracellularly to its active triphosphate form, which is then incorporated into the viral DNA chain, causing chain termination and effectively inhibiting the activity of the HIV reverse transcriptase and HBV polymerase.

RNA information: A designed Watson–Crick stem-loop RNA with a 7-bp stem and 4-nt loop (AGUG). It serves as a non-G4 control structure. Ligands 2a and 2b bind to it with lower affinity , confirming their specificity for G4 structures.
Small_molecule_information: 4,11-bis(2-aminoethylamino)naphtho[2,3-f][1]benzofuran-5,10-dione, also referred to as a furan-fused anthraquinone derivative with bis(2-aminoethylamino) side chains, is a compound based on a Naphthoquinone core fused with a Benzofuran ring, resulting in a Naphtho[2,3-f][1]benzofuran-5,10-dione structure, substituted with two 2-aminoethylamino groups at positions C-4 and C-11. Anthraquinone/Naphthoquinone compounds are a well-known class of DNA topoisomerase inhibitors and DNA intercalators, forming the basis of many chemotherapy drugs (e.g., Mitoxantrone). Additionally, these quinone structures readily undergo redox cycling, generating Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS) that cause DNA damage and cell apoptosis, and this compound also exhibits high cellular uptake, strong stabilization of KRAS RG4s and specific binding affinity, which enables it to effectively inhibit translation and induce apoptosis in pancreatic cancer cells, meaning it is therefore primarily researched as a novel anti-tumor agent with a mechanism involving dual DNA intercalation and ROS-mediated cytotoxicity.

RNA information: pre-miR-21 is a stem-loop non-coding RNA precursor with a distinct structure, containing G28-A29 (Dicer-TRBP cleavage site) in the stem and single-stranded bulges/apical loops (small-molecule binding pockets). It processes into oncogenic mature miR-21; ligands like 4a or bPGN  bind specifically, inhibiting Dicer processing, upregulating tumor suppressors (PDCD4, PTEN), and suppressing cancer cell proliferation. It is a druggable target for cancers such as colorectal cancer.
Small_molecule_information: N-(2,3-dihydro-1H-pyrrolo[3,4-c]pyridin-6-ylmethyl)-N-methylaniline, also referred to as 4b, is a relatively simple amine heterocyclic compound, consisting of an N-methylaniline moiety (a key moiety for pre-miR-21 binding) linked to a 2,3-dihydro-1H-pyrrolo[3,4-c]pyridine group via a methylene bridge, and this 2-amino-dihydropyrrolopyridine scaffold-based small molecule is synthesized via a sustainable, modular ruthenium-catalyzed [222] cycloaddition route, which shows good pre-miR-21 selectivity vs. duplex DNA/tRNA but lacks Dicer cleavage inhibition activity likely due to the absence of crucial H-bond interactions from its aniline group; as a tertiary amine, it is easily protonated at physiological pH and exhibits moderate lipophilicity, and this combination of properties suggests good membrane permeability and potential for traversing the blood-brain barrier, while in real-world applications, it is often explored as a candidate for Monoamine Oxidase (MAO) inhibition or as a ligand for specific neurotransmitter receptors, making it relevant for the treatment of neurodegenerative diseases like Parkinsons or conditions like depression.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: 1,2-dimethoxy-12-methyl-[1,3]benzodioxolo[5,6-c]phenanthridin-12-ium, also referred to as Chelerythrine, is a methylated benzophenanthridine quaternary alkaloid featuring a rigid planar aromatic system and a permanent positive charge, and it is also a natural nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound that exhibits significant structural and functional properties when interacting with Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA; it can specifically bind to the binding pocket of 108yjdF RNA, inducing significant changes in RNA structure which are highly similar to those when binding to other nitrogen-containing aromatic compounds such as proflavine, indicating that the binding pocket of 108yjdF RNA has a broad recognition ability for such compounds, and it has a relatively high binding affinity for 108yjdF RNA, which indicates that its interaction with RNA is very strong, in addition, it can also activate gene expression regulated by 108yjdF RNA, which further confirms its functional role in gene regulation, and although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, it, as one of the potential ligands, provides important clues for studying the functional mechanism of this riboswitch; in real-world pharmacology, these structures are notorious for their potent DNA intercalation properties, where they insert themselves between base pairs, disrupting DNA structure and function, this mechanism makes them effective cytotoxic agents with powerful anti-tumor and anti-proliferative activity by interfering with DNA replication and Topoisomerase activity, and it has also been reported as an inhibitor of Multidrug Resistance-associated Proteins (MRPs), offering potential value in overcoming cancer resistance mechanisms.

RNA information: The r(CCUG)₁₀ sequence models the pathogenic RNA associated with myotonic dystrophy type 2. Structurally, it contains four-nucleotide repeats that also produce hairpins with mismatches, although their geometry differs slightly from CUG repeats. These motifs create similar alternating stripes of electrostatic potential, making selectivity between CUG and CCUG challenging for both proteins and small molecules. Functionally, r(CCUG)₁₀ sequesters MBNL1 in a manner comparable to CUG repeats, contributing to splicing defects. Its inclusion in binding studies demonstrates the difficulty of achieving discrimination between closely related repeat RNAs and emphasizes the need for ligands that exploit subtle structural differences.
Small_molecule_information: (Z,2S,7S)-N,N-bis[(2S)-6-amino-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxohexan-2-yl]-2,7-bis[[(2S)-1-(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)pyrrolidine-2-carbonyl]amino]oct-4-enediamide, also referred to as DCC 42012, is the cis (Z) isomer of (E,2S,7S)-N,N-bis[(2S)-6-amino-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxohexan-2-yl]-2,7-bis[[(2S)-1-(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)pyrrolidine-2-carbonyl]amino]oct-4-enediamide, a benzo[g]quinoline-containing Z-isomer designed through olefin metathesis, with the double bond in the core oct-4-enediamide scaffold having the cis configuration, and its geometry places the heteroaromatic moiety in an orientation that promotes favorable stacking interactions with RNA bases, compared to earlier disulfide-linked scaffolds, the olefin bioisostere of this compound improves chemical stability and compatibility for cellular studies, structurally, it exemplifies the importance of precise spatial placement of aromatic and peptide elements to achieve cooperative binding across repeat sequences, functionally, it enters cells efficiently and demonstrates biological activity by alleviating RNA-mediated toxicity in model systems, making it a strong lead structure for RNA-targeted small-molecule drug design, this conformational difference introduces a fixed bend in the molecules three-dimensional structure, in complex peptidomimetics, such cis-trans isomerization is often the decisive factor for biological activity, as it affects the precise spatial matching with the target binding pocket, the compound maintains the poly-cationic and highly aromatic features of its counterpart, its potential applications are similar to those of peptidomimetics, but the activity and selectivity profile of (Z,2S,7S)-N,N-bis[(2S)-6-amino-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxohexan-2-yl]-2,7-bis[[(2S)-1-(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)pyrrolidine-2-carbonyl]amino]oct-4-enediamide will be significantly altered due to the cis conformation, making it a critical tool for studying the impact of molecular conformation on biological activity.

RNA information: The r(CAG)₁₀ sequence serves as a comparative motif to test ligand selectivity against repeat RNAs with A–A mismatches instead of U–U mismatches. Structurally, it forms A-form helical RNA with alternating positive and negative electrostatic stripes similar to CUG repeats, leading to comparable overall architectures. Functionally, r(CAG)₁₀ is not associated with myotonic dystrophy but provides a stringent control for assessing molecular recognition. Because many proteins and synthetic ligands show similar affinity toward both CUG and CAG repeats, this sequence demonstrates the challenges of engineering true sequence selectivity in RNA-targeted small-molecule design.
Small_molecule_information: (E,2S,7S)-N,N-bis[(2S)-6-amino-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxohexan-2-yl]-2,7-bis[[(2S)-2-(propylamino)propanoyl]amino]oct-4-enediamide, also referred to as DCC 7 (2012), is a symmetric oligo-peptide analogue featuring multiple stereocenters and an internal (E)-alkene double bond within its oct-4-enediamide core, and it is the E-isomer analog of compound 6 that lacks the benzo[g]quinoline group; this type of peptidic structure, incorporating non-natural amino acid fragments and a geometrically constrained core, is typically pursued as a candidate for proteinase inhibitor or a G-protein coupled receptor (GPCR) ligand, while the absence of the aromatic moiety in this compound (relative to related structures) leads to only modest binding and a complete loss of selectivity among different RNA sequences, and structurally its configuration affects backbone orientation but does not compensate for the missing heteroaromatic system, functionally it shows minimal or no activity in biological assays (reinforcing the observation that effective RNA recognition requires both the aromatic stacking element and appropriate stereochemistry) and serves primarily as a comparative tool in evaluating structurefunction dependencies, meanwhile the rigid E-configuration (trans) of the alkene is critical for locking the molecule into a specific conformation, which is paramount for achieving the necessary molecular recognition and high-affinity binding at the proteins active site, and due to its multiple basic amine groups, the compound is likely polycationic at physiological pH, suggesting potential use in areas like antiviral, anti-inflammatory, or immunomodulatory therapies.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: 17-methoxy-N-(3-piperidin-1-ylpropyl)-5,7-dioxa-13-azoniapentacyclo[11.8.0.0{2,10}.0{4,8}.0{15,20}]henicosa-1(13),2,4(8),9,14,16,18,20-octaen-16-amine chloride The side chain of this molecule terminates with a piperidine ring attached via a three-carbon chain to the cationic core. Piperidine, a six-membered saturated amine, is one of the most common pharmacophores in medicinal chemistry, used to increase lipophilicity and tune conformation. In clinical practice, piperidine scaffolds are found in antipsychotics, antihistamines, and cardiovascular drugs. The combination of the bulky quaternary core and the piperidine terminus suggests that it may act as a voltage-gated ion channel modulator (e.g., a potassium channel blocker) or a potent Multidrug Resistance (MDR) reversal agent by interfering with cell membrane transporter function to enhance chemotherapy efficacy.

RNA information: 5'CCG/3'GCC is a 1×1 CG internal-loop RNA motif of the 5'CNG/3'GNC family, with a short stem loop (5'CCG/3'GCC), no U·U mismatches, G-C/C-G pairing, high stability, and low "breathing effects". Pathogenically linked to FXTAS (via mutant FMR1 mRNA’s r(CCG) repeats), it acts as a disease-specific RNA switch, recruiting proteins/ligands (e.g., diguanidinobenzoates) to modulate splicing/RAN translation. It also serves as an experimental control for RNA-small molecule binding specificity.
Small_molecule_information: 8-(9-carboxy-4-formyl-1,3,6-trihydroxyphenazin-2-yl)-6-formyl-4,7,9-trihydroxyphenazine-1-carboxylic acid, also referred to as Dilomofungin, is an extremely complex dimeric molecule, consisting of two highly substituted phenazine units linked by a carbon-carbon bond, which is a spontaneous dimer of lomofungin formed in DMSO and structurally features a carboncarbon bond between two lomofungin monomers. The phenazine scaffold is a heterocyclic system made of two benzene rings fused with two nitrogen atoms, and many natural phenazine derivatives (like pyocyanin) are pigments produced by microorganisms with potent antibiotic and biological redox activities. This compound possesses multiple functional groups, including hydroxyl, carboxyl, and formyl groups, which lend the molecule multiple hydrogen bond donor/acceptor capabilities and high water solubility, and it exhibits higher potency than lomofungin in inhibiting MBNL1-CUG RNA binding in vitro, while also having strong binding affinity and the ability to partially rescue mis-splicing in DM1 cell models, though it has the unintended effect of stabilizing toxic CUG repeat RNA, leading to its accumulation in nuclear foci and reduced transcript turnover, which limits its therapeutic potential for myotonic dystrophy type 1. The phenazine rings establish it as a potent redox-active molecule capable of mediating electron transfer in biological systems. In reality, such structurally complex phenazine derivatives are often explored as novel antibiotics or anticancer agents, where the mechanism of action might involve DNA intercalation or the generation of reactive oxygen species to induce apoptosis. This molecule could also serve as a chelating agent or redox indicator in biochemical analysis.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: 17-methoxy-N-(2-morpholin-4-ylethyl)-5,7-dioxa-13-azoniapentacyclo[11.8.0.0{2,10}.0{4,8}.0{15,20}]henicosa-1(13),2,4(8),9,14,16,18,20-octaen-16-amine chloride The side chain of this compound is 2-morpholin-4-ylethyl. The morpholine-ethylamine backbone is a significant motif in drug design, often used to create molecules with optimized membrane permeability and specific enzyme inhibition profiles. In practice, morpholine-ethylamine derivatives are frequently found in kinase inhibitors (targeting pathways like EGFR, VEGFR) and antiviral agents. This structure allows for effective interaction with both hydrophilic and hydrophobic regions of a target. Its quaternary ammonium nature suggests that it might exhibit antimicrobial or antineoplastic potential in addition to any kinase inhibitory properties.

Small_molecule_information: (E,2S,7S)-2,7-bis[[(2S)-6-amino-2-[(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)amino]hexanoyl]amino]-N-[(2S)-1-(2-aminoethylamino)-1-oxopropan-2-yl]-N-[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxopropan-2-yl]-N,N-dipropyloct-4-enediamide, also referred to as DCC 9 (2012), is a highly complex asymmetric peptidomimetic bearing the benzo[g]quinoline moiety; while benzo[g]quinoline is a necessary aromatic element for relevant interactions, DCC 9 (2012) has an altered peptide framework that disrupts the orientation required for strong and selective recognition, resulting in a weaker interaction profile, limited ability to discriminate among target sequences, and minimal biological effects, which confirms that benzo[g]quinoline is necessary but not sufficient and that correct positioning within the molecular scaffold is equally critical, highlighting its role as a negative control and supporting evidence for the structureactivity relationship developed across the compound series. The molecules core eight-carbon chain contains a trans (E) double bond, and its most significant feature is the unequal length of the polyamine chains attached to the two termini: one is a 3-aminopropylamine (three carbons) and the other is a 2-aminoethylamine (two carbons); this asymmetric polyamine structure is designed to potentially cause differences in binding affinity or mode with biological targets, and the molecule is symmetrically linked to benzo[g]quinoline-lysine derivatives on both sides, integrating DNA intercalation capability with polycationic electrostatic binding, with this design combining asymmetry and dual-targeting groups aiming to investigate how regulating asymmetric conformation can enhance the selectivity and multi-action efficacy against specific biological targets, holding potential application in multi-target anti-cancer therapy.

These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: (E)-3-phenylprop-2-enoic acid, also referred to as Cinnamic acid, is an aromatic unsaturated carboxylic acid with the formula C9H8O2; it is an unsaturated fatty acid characterized by a benzene ring (i.e., phenyl group) connected to a vinyl group and a carboxyl group, with its structure specifically consisting of a phenyl group connected to a carboxylic acid group via a trans (E) double bond. Cinnamic acid occurs naturally in plants, particularly in cinnamon bark and storax, and acts as an important biosynthetic precursor in plants for various secondary metabolites like flavonoids, lignin, and coumarins. In practical applications, Cinnamic acid and its esters are known for their pleasant, characteristic aroma, making them widely used in the flavoring, perfume, and food industries. Furthermore, its conjugated structure allows it to absorb UV radiation, leading to its use as an ingredient in sunscreens to protect the skin from UV damage. It and its derivatives are also investigated for certain antimicrobial, antifungal, and anti-inflammatory activities. In terms of its interaction with 108yjdF RNA, Cinnamic acid does not have a strong binding affinity and does not induce gene expression, implying that it may not be an effective ligand for 108yjdF RNA.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 4-methylbenzenesulfonate;(2Z)-3-methyl-2-[(1-methylquinolin-1-ium-4-yl)methylidene]-1,3-benzothiazole, also referred to as Thiazole Orange, is an organic salt containing quinolinium and benzothiazole heterocyclic rings that intercalates into DNA and RNA, commonly used in flow cytometry and molecular biology for detecting nucleic acids; its structure is based on the quinoline and benzothiazole parent skeletons: a benzene ring is fused with a pyridine ring to form the quinoline skeleton, and a benzene ring is fused with a thiazole ring to form the benzothiazole skeleton, with these two parent nuclei connected by a methine group. In terms of biological function, this class of compounds typically functions as dyes, photosensitizers, or exhibits specific biological enzyme inhibitory activities (Benzothiazoles are important in therapeutic chemistry).

RNA information: The HIV-1 frameshift-stimulating stem-loop (FSS) RNA is a highly conserved structured element located within the gag-pol transcript that regulates production of the essential Gag-Pol polyprotein through a −1 ribosomal frameshift. It consists of a stable stem-loop positioned downstream of a slippery sequence that induces ribosomal pausing and promotes frameshifting in about 5–10% of translation events. This process allows translation to continue beyond the gag stop codon, producing Gag-Pol proteins such as protease, reverse transcriptase, and integrase that are required for infectious virion assembly. Altering FSS activity disrupts the Gag to Gag-Pol ratio, substantially reducing viral replication and infectivity.
Small_molecule_information: N-[(2S)-1-[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-4-[4-[(2S)-3-[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-2-[(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)-methylamino]-3-oxopropyl]triazol-1-yl]-1-oxobutan-2-yl]-2-ethyl-N-methylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carboxamide, confirms the impact of full N-methylation on potency, its structure features two units containing (2S)-phenylalaninyl-3-aminopropylamine and (2S)-proline fragments linked by a 1,4-disubstituted 1,2,3-triazole ring, with both termini acylated by a 2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl group, similar to RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: 5-methyl-7-[2-(4-methylmorpholin-4-ium-4-yl)ethoxy]-N-(2-morpholin-4-ylethyl)-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-amine diiodide. This is a dicationic benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium derivative with diiodide ions. Its dicationic structure includes the core N-methylation and the 4-methylmorpholin-4-ium group at position 7, along with a 2-morpholin-4-ylethylamino substituted chain at position 11. This structure specifically incorporates two morpholine groups into the molecule. The compound has practical potential in bio-probes and drug delivery vehicles. As a therapeutic agent, its cationic and polycyclic properties allow it to act as a DNA damaging agent or a chemotherapy sensitizer, enhancing the efficacy of existing chemotherapeutic drugs by binding to tumor cell DNA or inhibiting key proteins related to DNA metabolism.

Small_molecule_information: N-[7-[(1,1-dimethylpiperidin-1-ium-4-yl)methoxy]-5-methyl-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-yl]-N,N-diethylethane-1,2-diamine diiodide is a dicationic benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline quaternary ammonium salt with a diethylamine side chain. This structure is typical of DNA or RNA binding agents and is studied as a fluorescent probe and a candidate for anticancer/antibacterial drugs due to its potential to inhibit topoisomerases or act as a nucleic acid ligand.

RNA information: (5'CNG/3'CNG) denotes repetitive RNA sequences (N = any nucleotide) commonly in 5'/3' UTRs. Structurally, it forms stable hairpin or G-quadruplex structures. Functionally, it regulates post-transcriptional gene expression. Pathogenically, expanded subtypes (e.g., CUG/CAG repeats) cause microsatellite expansion disorders like myotonic dystrophy type 1, sequestering RNA-binding proteins (e.g., MBNL1) to disrupt alternative splicing, underpinning disease pathogenesis.
Small_molecule_information: (2E,4E)-5-(1,3-benzodioxol-5-yl)-1-piperidin-1-ylpenta-2,4-dien-1-one, also referred to as Piperine (a major bioactive alkaloid and phytochemical compound derived from black pepper (Piper nigrum) that is responsible for the pungent taste of pepper and has key characteristics including low water solubility and poor bioavailability), is a compound that is either a natural product derivative or a structural analogue, typically categorized with compounds related to Piperine. Its structure is characterized by a 1,3-benzodioxol-5-yl group (a signature motif derived from Piperine and common in natural products), linked via a conjugated diene bridge (composed of two double bonds) to a piperidin-1-yl-ketone (amide) moiety. This structure is highly planar and possesses an extended conjugated system, often resulting in a colored solid with potential unique fluorescence properties. Piperine and its derivatives exhibit various pharmacological activities, including anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, analgesic effects, and are most renowned for their bioavailability enhancing properties by inhibiting certain metabolizing enzymes like CYP3A4, with Piperine itself pharmacologically renowned for its enhancer effect, significantly increasing the absorption and efficacy of various drugs and nutrients by inhibiting digestive enzymes and drug-metabolizing enzymes like cytochrome P450, and also possessing intrinsic anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and potential neuroprotective properties; the specific piperidine substitution in (2E,4E)-5-(1,3-benzodioxol-5-yl)-1-piperidin-1-ylpenta-2,4-dien-1-one increases its lipophilicity, facilitating its passage across cell membranes, and it is often studied as a potential active component of natural product-based drugs or as an adjuvant to enhance the absorption of other pharmaceuticals.

RNA information: The influenza A virus RNA promoter features a bent A-form helical structure with a dynamic (A–A)–U internal loop that serves as a specific binding site for the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp) and nucleoprotein complex. This unique structural motif exhibits conformational flexibility that facilitates recognition by viral replication machinery while maintaining overall A-form geometry. The promoter's distinctive architecture and dynamic properties are essential for initiating viral RNA synthesis, making it a critical functional element in the influenza replication cycle and a promising target for antiviral therapeutic development; the influenza A virus RNA promoter is a highly conserved structural element located at the 3' and 5' ends of each viral RNA segment. Its structure, determined by NMR spectroscopy, adopts a panhandle-like conformation characterized by an A-form helix. A key structural feature is a unique (A-A)-U internal loop and a C-A mismatch pair. Furthermore, the promoter exhibits a significant bend of approximately 46 ± 10° near the viral RNA initiation site. Functionally, this specific architecture is specifically recognized by the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp), which binds to it to initiate both transcription and replication of the viral genome. The high conservation of its sequence across strains and its intolerance to mutations, which are detrimental to viral replication, establish the influenza RNA promoter as a novel and promising druggable target for antiviral development.; the influenza A virus RNA promoter is a highly conserved structural element located at the 3' and 5' ends of each viral RNA segment. Small_molecule_information: 1-[4-(4-amino-6,7-dimethoxyquinazolin-2-yl)piperazin-1-yl]propan-1-one, also referred to as Compound 6, is a compound containing quinazoline and piperazine structures, which is a derivative of compound 1 where the piperazine secondary amine is modified with a propionyl group (R Et); its structure is based on the quinazoline parent skeleton, substituted at C2 with a piperazinyl group, where the piperazine N1 is further substituted with a propionyl group, this structure is related to the Prazosin class of quinazolines, it binds to the influenza RNA promoter and exhibits antiviral activity with no cytotoxicity, resulting in a favorable IC/CC ratio, and in terms of biological function, quinazoline compounds generally exhibit diverse biological activities such as -receptor blockade (antihypertensive), antitumor, and related pharmacological effects.

RNA information: A10-RNA-Wt is a guanine (G)-rich sequence located in the 5' untranslated region (UTR) of ADAM10 mRNA, which can form a stable RNA G-quadruplex secondary structure. As a translational inhibitory element, this structure negatively regulates the protein synthesis of ADAM10 by adopting a specific spatial conformation that hinders ribosome binding or scanning. Studies have shown that targeting this structure with antisense oligonucleotides and small-molecule compounds (such as compound 24) can relieve its translational repression effect, up-regulate ADAM41 expression, thereby promoting the production of neuroprotective sAPP伪 and reducing the generation of peptides. This provides a novel strategy for the treatment of Alzheimer's disease.
Small_molecule_information: 9-ethyl-3-[(E)-2-(1-methylquinolin-1-ium-2-yl)ethenyl]carbazole, also referred to as Compound 16, is a simpler carbazole-quinoline fluorescent dye with high quantum yield, which is a 1-methylquinolinium derivative with a (9-ethyl-9H-carbazol-2-yl)vinyl group at the 2-position (no 4-position amine substitution) synthesized via general procedure B, binds ADAM10 mRNAs QGRS with moderate affinity and low mutant RNA interaction, weakly up-regulates ADAM10 translation in dual-luciferase assays and is less potent than analogs with 4-methylpiperazine due to lacking this motif for optimal RNA G-quadruplex binding, and is used in applications like dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) or as biological probes for cell membranes.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: 5-methylphenazin-1-one, also referred to as pyocyanin, this compound has a Phenazine structure; pyocyanin is a planar azaaromatic phenazine derivative with a fused bicyclic heterocyclic core (two benzene rings linked by two nitrogen atoms) and hydroxyl/methoxy groups, which binds to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) via non-specific interactions but with lower affinity than potent ligands like chelerythrine, fails to activate riboswitch-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis while naturally acting as a virulence factor in Pseudomonas aeruginosa and aiding in studying riboswitch ligand structure-activity relationships, and Phenazine compounds generally possess broad biological activities, including antibacterial, antifungal, and anticancer effects, and may be developed as new antibiotics or anticancer drugs.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: 3-(4-fluorophenyl)-2,5-dimethyl-7H-pyrrolo[2,3-d]pyrimidin-1-ium-4-imine, also referred to as CP1 which features an imidazopyrimidine fused heterocyclic core with multiple nitrogen atoms, substituted by two methyl groups and a 4-fluorophenyl ring, is based on the 7H-pyrrolo[2,3-d]pyrimidine core, which is an important purine nucleobase analog (7-deazaguanine); the molecule has an unusual electronic structure, being quaternized at N1 and featuring an imine (CN) at C4, forming a 1-ium-4-imine system, and it is substituted with a 4-fluorophenyl group at C3 and methyl groups at C2 and C5, while its binding data showed higher affinity for the control RNA, indicating a lack of specificity for the pathogenic AA mismatch motif, which limited its further development as a lead compound, and this unique ionic and imine-containing nucleobase analog makes 3-(4-fluorophenyl)-2,5-dimethyl-7H-pyrrolo[2,3-d]pyrimidin-1-ium-4-imine an excellent candidate for antimetabolites or enzyme inhibitors that interfere with nucleic acid biosynthesis or metabolism, and it may be designed as a novel antiviral or anti-cancer agent by mimicking natural substrates.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GAC)x6: This RNA contains six consecutive CAG repeats with AA mismatches, forming multiple internal loops. It is a key target for small molecules due to its structural similarity to pathogenic expanded repeats. It shows high affinity and selective binding to compounds such as pyridocoumarin derivatives, leading to conformational changes and thermal stabilization.; The (5'CAG/3'GAC)x6 RNA contains multiple consecutive A-A mismatch loops. This multi-loop structure enhances conformational stability and ligand binding avidity, more accurately mimicking the expanded toxic repeats found in patient-derived RNA and providing a robust platform for biophysical testing.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-2-benzoyl-9-bromo-4-phenylchromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one, also referred to as Compound 14, is a four-ring heterocycle based on the chromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one scaffold, the molecule is substituted with a phenyl group at 4, a bromo atom at 9, and a benzoyl group at 2, compared to some of its analogues, this compound lacks the para-fluoro atom on the benzoyl group at position 2, this defluorination is a common optimization step in drug discovery used to finely tune the molecules electronic distribution and lipophilicity, due to its inherent planarity and multiple aromatic rings, the structure of this compound suggests it is a potential DNA intercalator or small molecule inhibitor, in research, similar large planar heterocyclic ketones are often explored for their therapeutic potential against proliferative diseases, achieved through high-affinity binding to key intracellular targets, and this compound which contains a bromine atom showed high binding affinity to r(CAG)exp RNA, was effective in biophysical assays and reduced polyQ aggregation in cells.

RNA information: 5'CGG/3'GGC is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to fragile X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome (FXTAS), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it forms a hairpin with a 1×1 internal loop, closed by CG pairs, adopting non-canonical folding. Functionally, it contributes to FXTAS pathogenesis, likely by sequestering cellular proteins and disrupting normal splicing. As a disease-relevant RNA, it serves as a target for screening therapeutic small molecules.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[(7-chloroquinolin-4-yl)amino]-6-(diethylaminomethyl)phenol, also referred to as Amodiaquine Di HCl, is a quinoline derivative structured around a quinoline core, with a chloro substitution at the 7-position and an amino link at the 4-position to a benzene ring containing a phenolic hydroxyl group and a diethylaminomethyl group (molecular formula C20H22ClN3O), and it is an antimalarial drug with immunomodulatory effects that shares mechanisms with chloroquine but has higher toxicity risks, limiting its use despite efficacy against resistant Plasmodium strains; this compound possesses a planar conjugated system and multiple functional groups (chloro, amine, phenol, quinoline), functionally, the quinoline core is famous as the scaffold for well-known antimalarial drugs (such as chloroquine), and derivatives are commonly investigated for broad-spectrum antimicrobial, antimalarial, and anti-cancer activities, the presence of the phenolic hydroxyl and amine groups suggests potential for metal complexation or binding to enzyme active sites.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC)x20: This is a control RNA with twenty repeats of perfectly paired CAG-GUC duplex. It is used to contrast with the mismatched x20 RNA and shows negligible binding and structural changes, underscoring the importance of the AA mismatch in selective compound recognition.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-9-bromo-2-(4-methoxybenzoyl)-4-phenylchromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one, also referred to as Compound 15, is a highly substituted chromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one, and Compound 15 was identified as a top lead compound with very high affinity and exceptional selectivity for r(CAG)exp RNA, the chromene moiety is utilized in antiviral and antitumor drug design, the presence of a bromo atom, amino group, and benzoyl group suggests it may act as a potent enzyme inhibitor or receptor antagonist, potentially blocking key processes in cancer cell proliferation, its bromine substituent enhanced RNA binding through additional halogen bonding, it significantly reduced polyQ aggregates, was cell-permeable, and showed low toxicity. RNA

RNA information: 5′UAG/3′A_C (SL I): The UAG/A_C motif is located within HCV 3′ UTR stem–loop I. Identified in the Inforna analysis as a ligandable element, it was enriched in binding assays, indicating its potential as a structural determinant for small molecule recognition and functional viral RNA inhibition.
Small_molecule_information: 1-N,4-N-bis[3,5-bis(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl]benzene-1,4-dicarboxamide, also referred to as Compound 9, is a highly symmetrical polyamine compound built upon a terephthalamide (benzene-1,4-dicarboxamide) core, its notable characteristic is that each side chain phenyl ring bears two 4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl (imidazolinyl) groups at positions 3 and 5, with two such substituted phenyl groups attached to the core, the entire molecule contains a total of four imidazolinyl groups, all of which are strong bases and can be protonated at physiological pH, making it a potent tetracationic system, this structural design, concentrating multiple positive charges on an aromatic scaffold, is a classic strategy for developing DNA minor groove binders, due to the NH groups of the imidazolinyl moieties serving as hydrogen bond donors, the molecule interacts with the minor groove of DNA through a combination of electrostatic forces, hydrogen bonding, and van der Waals interactions, such tetracationic molecules are studied in medicinal chemistry as antifungal, antiprotozoal (e.g., antileishmanial), and anticancer agents, and this compound served as a negative control in RNA binding and antiviral assays, it did not exhibit significant binding to the enriched RNA motifs identified in the combinatorial screening, in agreement, it failed to interact with HCV 3 UTR stemloops I and II and showed no measurable inhibition of viral replication in cellular assays, its inactivity in both in vitro and in-cell experiments validated the specificity of Compounds 7, 8, and 10 for targeting HCV RNA motifs, demonstrating that not all small molecules engage structured RNA with functional consequences.

RNA information: B. The 5 '-UTR of subtilis pbuE contains a 70-nt adenine-sensing ribose switch: its secondary structure is composed of conserved three-stem junction regions (P1-P3), where the P1 helix determines stability, and the ligand binding site is located in the core pocket; When transcription progresses to approximately the 80th to 110th nucleotide, if adenine binds, aptamer folds to prevent the formation of the downstream terminator (63-79 pairs with 83-98 bases), RNA polymerase reads through it, initiates pbuE gene expression, and generates an adenine effloression pump to resist purine toxicity. Otherwise, the terminator folds rapidly and the transcription terminates prematurely. The regulation of this switch is jointly controlled by adenine binding kinetics and the transcriptional time scale, and it can switch between kinetic and thermodynamic modes.
Small_molecule_information: 7H-purin-6-amine, also referred to as Adenine (a nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound), is one of the four fundamental nucleobases that constitute nucleic acids (DNA and RNA), structured as a purine ring with an amino group at position 6, molecular formula C5H5N5. In terms of real-world activity, Adenine is a vital building block for life; it is an essential component of genetic material, pairing with Thymine (T) in the DNA double helix and Uracil (U) in RNA via hydrogen bonds, forming the basis for storing and expressing genetic information, is the core structure of the cells energy currency ATP (Adenosine Triphosphate), ADP, and AMP, participating in all major cellular energy transfer processes, and is a constituent of several important coenzymes (e.g., NAD, FAD), involved in a wide range of redox reactions and metabolic regulation; additionally, Adenine is embedded in the core of the pbuE ribose switch with a simple 6-aminopine plane, inducing local base rearrangement and stabilizing the P1 helix, rapidly occupies RNA within the instantaneous window of transcription, thereby inhibiting terminon folding and enabling the expression of downstream efference pump genes, and serves as a natural start button for bacteria to sense and excrete excess purine and maintain intracellular homeostasis.

RNA information: r(CUG)₁₂ (r(5′CUG/3′GUC)12) is a pathogenic RNA motif modeling myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1)-causing repeats. Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with 12 CUG repeats, featuring a C-G paired stem and periodic 1×1 UU internal loops that create specific binding pockets. Functionally, it sequesters MBNL1 splicing regulator to disrupt pre-mRNA splicing (DM1’s core pathogenesis), serves as a non-cognate control for binding specificity assays, and is a target for small molecules that displace MBNL1 to restore RNA processing.
Small_molecule_information: 1-N,4-N-bis[4-(diaminomethylideneamino)phenyl]benzene-1,4-dicarboxamide, also referred to as Compound P3, is a bis-guanidinated terephthalamide, structurally similar to the bis-amidines and a pentamidine-like small molecule identified through 3-D shape-based similarity screening as a next-generation ligand for the toxic r(CUG)exp RNA that causes myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1); it retains the twin benzamidine warheads of pentamidine but is decorated with an extended hydrophobic linker that increases its shape complementarity to the deep major groove of the 5CUG/3GUC hairpin repeat, and in a quantitative TR-FRET displacement assay it dissociates MBNL1 from r(CUG)12 with greater potency than pentamidine yet is less selective than P1, binding similarly to RNAs containing certain motifs and to the human A-site, while in DM1 cell models a specific dose of it partially shifts a bichromatic MBNL1 exon 5 reporter toward the normal exon-skipped DsRed signal though cytotoxicity becomes evident at higher doses, thus exemplifying how computational shape similarity can rapidly evolve an antimicrobial template into a low-micromolar chemical probe for DM1 RNA that may serve as a stepping-stone toward in-vivo-compatible DM1 therapeutics; its polycationic nature gives it DNA-binding potential, and it is often studied as a broad-spectrum antimicrobial agent, including antibacterial and antifungal applications, and is also a potential candidate for a serine protease inhibitor, but detailed biological activity data is lacking.

RNA information: The r(CUG)₁₀ sequence represents a pathogenic-length model for expanded CUG repeats and folds into a stable hairpin with regularly spaced U–U mismatches. Structurally, it provides multiple equivalent binding sites, enabling one ligand to associate with each trinucleotide repeat and producing cooperative binding. This repeat length captures essential features of toxic RNA found in myotonic dystrophy, including the ability to sequester MBNL1 and form nuclear foci. Functionally, r(CUG)₁₀ is the most relevant in vitro construct for evaluating selective small-molecule recognition, since its length allows the manifestation of cooperative interactions that mimic disease-relevant RNA–protein complexes.
Small_molecule_information: (Z,2S,7S)-2,7-bis[[(2S)-6-amino-2-[(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)amino]hexanoyl]amino]-N-[(2S)-1-(2-aminoethylamino)-1-oxopropan-2-yl]-N-[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxopropan-2-yl]-N,N-dipropyloct-4-enediamide, also referred to as DCC 8 (2012), is an asymmetric peptidomimetic with a cis (Z) double bond in its core eight-carbon chain, which forces the molecule into a rigid bent conformation; while DCC 8 (2012) contains the benzo[g]quinoline unit, it features a scrambled peptide sequence that alters the relative spatial positioning of functional groups, a design that tests whether the orientation of the heteroaromatic group and backbone is essential for recognition, and though the compound can interact with RNA, its selectivity is poor and its affinity reduced compared to ordered analogs, structurally demonstrating that sequence context is crucial for cooperative interactions and functionally failing to show strong biological rescue, thus serving as a control that validates the need for precise arrangement of components to achieve productive recognition and therapeutic activity, and this specific cis geometry of the main peptidomimetic is crucial for this molecule with asymmetric polyamine side chains (two- and three-carbon), as it determines the relative spatial position and distance of the two different side chains, thereby influencing target interaction, with both sides of the molecule also linked to benzo[g]quinoline-lysine groups, and it is a key molecule for studying the effect of conformational locking on the activity of asymmetric multifunctional molecules, whose target activity remains DNA binding and topoisomerase inhibition, but whose activity is expected to exhibit unique selectivity due to its characteristic Z-conformation.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 4-methylbenzenesulfonate;(2Z)-3-methyl-2-[(1-methylquinolin-1-ium-4-yl)methylidene]-1,3-benzothiazole, also referred to as Thiazole Orange, is an organic salt containing quinolinium and benzothiazole heterocyclic rings that intercalates into DNA and RNA, commonly used in flow cytometry and molecular biology for detecting nucleic acids; its structure is based on the quinoline and benzothiazole parent skeletons: a benzene ring is fused with a pyridine ring to form the quinoline skeleton, and a benzene ring is fused with a thiazole ring to form the benzothiazole skeleton, with these two parent nuclei connected by a methine group. In terms of biological function, this class of compounds typically functions as dyes, photosensitizers, or exhibits specific biological enzyme inhibitory activities (Benzothiazoles are important in therapeutic chemistry).

RNA information: r(CUG)₁₂ (r(5′CUG/3′GUC)12) is a pathogenic RNA motif modeling myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1)-causing repeats. Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with 12 CUG repeats, featuring a C-G paired stem and periodic 1×1 UU internal loops that create specific binding pockets. Functionally, it sequesters MBNL1 splicing regulator to disrupt pre-mRNA splicing (DM1’s core pathogenesis), serves as a non-cognate control for binding specificity assays, and is a target for small molecules that displace MBNL1 to restore RNA processing.
Small_molecule_information: N-[4-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl]-2-[[4-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl]carbamoylamino]acetamide, also referred to as Compound H9 (a bis-benzimidazole derivative of Hoechst 33258), is a molecule with a linear acetamide backbone and a central urea group, with both ends substituted by a phenyl ring bearing a 4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl (imidazolinyl) group, the imidazolinyl group is strongly basic and is almost fully protonated at physiological pH, making this compound a classic example of a polycationic molecule, the central urea (N(H)CON(H)) and amide (CON(H)) linkages provide multiple hydrogen bond donors and acceptors, due to its length, rigidity, and strong cationic nature, this molecule is designed as a nucleic acid binding agent, intended to interact tightly with the phosphate backbone of DNA or RNA via electrostatic forces, and possibly through intercalation between base pairs, such compounds are frequently investigated as broad-spectrum antimicrobial agents (especially antifungal and antiprotozoal) or as gene expression modulators, while Compound H9 retains the DNA-binding benzimidazole core of Hoechst 33258 but is elongated with a hydrophobic p-chlorophenyl tail that improves its shape complementarity to the major groove of the 5CUG/3GUC hairpin repeat, can dissociate MBNL1 from r(CUG)exp RNA (the pathogenic RNA causing myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1)) with greater potency than Hoechst 33258 though it is less selective than H1 as it binds with similar affinity to RNAs containing 5CUG/3GCC or 5CGG/3GGC motifs and to the human A-site, and in DM1 cell models it can partially shift a bichromatic MBNL1 exon 5 reporter toward the normal exon-skipped DsRed signal while showing cytotoxicity at higher doses, exemplifying how computational shape similarity can evolve a DNA-binding dye into a chemical probe for DM1 RNA that may serve as a stepping-stone toward in-vivo-compatible DM1 therapeutics.

RNA information: 5′GUG/3′C_C: This asymmetric loop motif was found both in HCV 3′ UTR stem–loop I and stem–loop III. It represents a recurrent viral structural element and was highlighted in Table 1 as a key binding site. Small molecules engaging this motif displayed antiviral activity, linking it to functional inhibition of HCV replication.; 5′UUA/3′A_U: This internal loop motif contains a three-nucleotide bulge opposite two nucleotides with a mismatch. It was identified as a preferred binding site for several small molecules, including Compounds 7 and 8, with submicromolar affinity. Functional analysis demonstrated that the motif occurs in HCV 3′ UTR stem–loops, where small molecule binding interferes with viral replication. Its asymmetric structure provides a selective recognition element for ligands designed to target RNA.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[[3-[[2,5-bis[[4-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl]carbamoyl]phenyl]carbamoylamino]phenyl]carbamoylamino]-1-N,4-N-bis[4-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl]benzene-1,4-dicarboxamide, also referred to as Compound 7, is an extremely large, highly branched polycationic compound, and it can selectively bind internal loop motifs such as 5UUA/3A_U and 5UGU/3G_A with submicromolar affinity, with structural studies revealing that its binding is driven by recognition of asymmetric bulges within RNA stems, it can directly interact with HCV 3 UTR stemloops I and II by targeting motifs present in viral RNA, functional assays have demonstrated that it inhibits HCV replication in cell culture without affecting host ribosomal RNAs, and RNase H/ASO mapping has confirmed its intracellular engagement of the predicted binding sites; its intricate scaffold contains multiple phenyl rings, amide bonds, and urea groups, terminated by several 4-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl substituents, structural analysis indicates that this molecule bears at least six imidazolinyl groups, making it a potent hexacationic system, this structural design is an extreme example used to construct supramolecular nucleic acid binders, where the molecular length, rigidity, and multiple charges enable high-affinity interactions with multiple sites on the DNA helix, including the minor groove, major groove, or phosphate backbone, and such compounds are often investigated as non-viral gene delivery vectors (by charge-neutralizing DNA), highly effective anticancer agents, or for use as DNA structural probes, aiming for extremely high binding stability and cytotoxicity.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a short, genetically conserved stem–bulge–loop hairpin found at the 5′ end of every viral transcript.  Functional constructs range from minimal 29-nt fragments (containing the essential bulge and loop) up to the canonical 52–59-nt forms seen in full-length genomes; in all cases the active fold is the same: a double-stranded stem interrupted by a three-base pyrimidine bulge (commonly UCU) and capped by a six-nucleotide loop.  This bulge-and-loop motif first captures an arginine-rich segment of the viral Tat protein, inducing a local conformational switch that allows Tat to recruit the host Cyclin T1–CDK9 (P-TEFb) complex.  The resultant Tat–TAR–P-TEFb assembly converts RNA polymerase II from abortive to highly processive elongation, boosting viral gene expression >100-fold, and the same RNA structure also facilitates strand-transfer during reverse transcription.  Because the bulge–loop signature is indispensable and sequence-invariant across strains, TAR acts as a natural “riboswitch-like” sensor that tunes the balance between transcription and genome replication, offering a structurally small yet functionally critical target for therapeutic intervention.
Small_molecule_information: 3,6-dihydroxy-6-isothiocyanatospiro[2-benzofuran-3,9-xanthene]-1-one, also referred to as Fluorescein Isothiocyanate Isomer I (FITC-I; CHNOS), shares the fluorescent dye scaffold with 4,5,6,7-tetrachloro-3,6-dihydroxyspiro[2-benzofuran-3,9-xanthene]-1-one, based on the core structure of fluorescein, which is spiro[2-benzofuran-3,9-xanthene]-1-one, and this synthetic, water-soluble xanthene probe originally designed for protein labeling has a rigid, planar chromophore and pendant isothiocyanate that make it a functional analog of the isoalloxazine ring of flavin mononucleotide (FMN), with its anionic ribityl phosphate replaced by a neutral but hydrogen-bond-competent isothiocyanate arm to gain membrane permeability and photochemical stability while preserving the aromatic stacking and H-bond donor/acceptor pattern required for RNA recognition; the unique feature of this compound is the presence of an isothiocyanato group (NCS) at the 6-position of the benzofuran ring, and this isothiocyanate is a highly reactive chemical group that can rapidly react with primary amine groups (NH2) on biomacromolecules (such as proteins, antibodies, or nucleic acids) to form stable thiourea linkages, while the compound can disrupt the TatTARcyclin T1 complex in electrophoretic mobility-shift assays and dose-dependently inhibit Tat-driven LTR trans-activation without affecting a heterologous Rev-RRE control in HeLa reporter assays, and NMR titrations reveal that it induces large chemical-shift perturbations exclusively in the 5 bulge even at sub-stoichiometric ratios, confirming RNA-specific recognition analogous to FMN-riboswitch binding; in real-world applications, this compound serves as a reactive fluorescent probe or fluorescent labeling reagent, and by covalently linking the fluorescein moiety to antibodies or other biological ligands via the isothiocyanate group, scientists can tag target biomolecules and subsequently use fluorescence microscopy or flow cytometry to visualize and quantify specific biological events inside and outside the cell, making it a standard tool in immunology, cell biology, and molecular biology research as well as a brightly fluorescent, readily conjugatable scaffold for developing next-generation antivirals that target viral RNA instead of host proteins.

RNA information: G4-EBNA1 is a stable G-quadruplex in the EBNA1 mRNA GAr region. It binds nucleolin (NCL), inhibiting translation and reducing antigen presentation to enable immune evasion. Its formation is length-dependent. Ligands like PhenDC3, PyDH2, and PhenDH2 disrupt NCL binding, restoring EBNA1 expression and antigen presentation. The structure prefers U-shaped ligands that stack on G-tetrads. Targeting G4-EBNA1 exposes infected cells to immune response, offering a therapeutic strategy against EBV.
Small_molecule_information: 4-N,6-N-bis[(E)-(1-methylquinolin-1-ium-4-yl)methylideneamino]pyrimidine-4,6-dicarboxamide diiodide, also referred to as PymDH2, is a dicationic compound with a pyrimidine-4,6-dicarboxamide core (containing a central pyrimidine core with 1-methylquinolinium lateral groups) linking two 1-methylquinolin-1-ium cationic groups via methylideneamino (imine) bonds, this structure is a complex, multicharged system with a rigid structure and four potential cationic centers, counterbalanced by two iodide ions, this polycationic nature gives it extremely high affinity for negatively charged biomolecules (such as DNA, ATP, etc.), it showed moderate G4 affinity but no effect on EBNA1 expression, in practical applications, it is studied as a nucleic acid probe and a therapeutic agent, binding to DNA through strong electrostatic interactions and stacking, particularly exhibiting high selectivity for Guanine-rich G-quadruplex structures, making it a potential anticancer G4 ligand.

RNA information: 5'CAG/3'CAG is a pathogenic RNA model linked to polyglutamine diseases like Huntington’s disease (HD) and spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs). Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with non-canonical A-A mismatches, creating unique structural pockets. Functionally, it mimics expanded CAG repeat-associated structural aberrations and serves as a high-affinity target for small molecules, representing key pathogenic RNA features in related neurodegenerative disorders.
Small_molecule_information: bis(2-acetamidoacetic acid);4-[2-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)iminohydrazinyl]benzenecarboximidamide, also referred to as Diminazine, is a complex salt molecule consisting of a large cationic formamidine hydrazonebased diamidine species and two 2-acetamidoacetic acid anions, and the ,-diamidine core connected through a biphenyl and hydrazone bridge corresponds to the class of aromatic diamidine compounds used as antiprotozoal agents that exert activity by binding to the minor groove of DNA, with such cationic diamidines commonly displaying antiprotozoal, antifungal, antiviral, anticancer, or broad-spectrum antimicrobial properties.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a short, genetically conserved stem–bulge–loop hairpin found at the 5′ end of every viral transcript.  Functional constructs range from minimal 29-nt fragments (containing the essential bulge and loop) up to the canonical 52–59-nt forms seen in full-length genomes; in all cases the active fold is the same: a double-stranded stem interrupted by a three-base pyrimidine bulge (commonly UCU) and capped by a six-nucleotide loop.  This bulge-and-loop motif first captures an arginine-rich segment of the viral Tat protein, inducing a local conformational switch that allows Tat to recruit the host Cyclin T1–CDK9 (P-TEFb) complex.  The resultant Tat–TAR–P-TEFb assembly converts RNA polymerase II from abortive to highly processive elongation, boosting viral gene expression >100-fold, and the same RNA structure also facilitates strand-transfer during reverse transcription.
