RNA information: RNA1 is a structured RNA oligonucleotide derived from the SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5 region, engineered to contain a single G bulge. It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: 3-(8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl)-7-[(3S)-3-methylpiperazin-4-ium-1-yl]-1H-quinolin-2-one, also referred to as SMSM73, is a non-planar coumarin analog bearing a 4-methylpiperazine A-ring and an imidazopyridine-thiazole DE-fused system, carrying a 1 charge, which recognizes the single-G bulge of RNA1 driven by minor-groove H-bonds, -stacking and electrostatics, as confirmed by fluorescence-polarization assays and SAR comparison, serving as a selective RNA ligand.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-3,5,7-trihydroxychromen-4-one, also referred to as Quercetin, is a natural flavonol compound and one of the most common and widely studied flavonoids in nature, with the formula C15H10O7. Its structure features hydroxyl groups on the A ring (5 and 7) and C ring (3), and a catechol moiety (two adjacent hydroxyl groups) on the B ring (3 and 4 positions). Quercetin is abundant in various plant-based foods, including onions, apples, tea, berries, and red wine. In real-world biological activity, Quercetin exhibits outstanding pharmacological properties: it is a powerful antioxidant and free-radical scavenger, effective at inhibiting lipid peroxidation, and also possesses anti-inflammatory and anticancer properties; it also acts as an effective anti-inflammatory agent by inhibiting histamine release, modulating immune cell function, and suppressing various inflammation-related enzymes (such as cyclooxygenase and lipoxygenase), and has been shown to interact with G-quadruplex DNA and may modulate RNA-related pathways. Furthermore, Quercetin is studied for its anti-allergic, cardioprotective, antiviral, and potential anticancer properties, and is widely used and researched as a dietary supplement or a component in natural medicine.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: 5-methyl-7-[2-(4-methylmorpholin-4-ium-4-yl)ethoxy]-N-(2-piperidin-1-ylethyl)-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-amine diiodide. This is a dicationic benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium derivative with diiodide ions, featuring a 4-methylmorpholin-4-ium quaternary ammonium side chain and a 2-piperidin-1-ylethyl substituted chain. The molecule structurally integrates two different saturated heterocycles (morpholine and piperidine). The potent cytotoxicity is a key feature of this class of compounds. In practical applications, it is regarded as a potential broad-spectrum germicide and antitumor agent. Its mechanism involves complex interactions with biological membranes, DNA, and RNA, with the quaternary ammonium structures being particularly important for its antibacterial activity against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: N,N-dimethyl-N-[5-methyl-7-[2-(4-methylmorpholin-4-ium-4-yl)ethoxy]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-yl]propane-1,3-diamine diiodide, this compound is a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline derivative with long diamine and quaternary ammonium salt side chains. The presence of the quaternary ammonium salt typically confers higher polarity and DNA binding affinity, making it a potential potent anti-tumor agent or a fluorescent probe in biological research.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 2,3,9,10-tetramethoxy-5,6-dihydroisoquinolino[2,1-b]isoquinolin-7-ium;chloride;hydrate, also referred to as Palmatine Chloride Hydrate, is a protoberberine alkaloid with antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and potential anticancer effects that interacts with DNA/RNA and modulates signaling pathways, and this molecule is a structural analog of the well-known natural product Berberine, belonging to the protoberberine class of alkaloids, characterized by its tetramethoxy substitution and quaternary ammonium structure; its natural origin and broad bioactivity make it a candidate for drug development, and the real-world pharmacological activities of this class are extensive: Berberine is widely used for its natural anti-diabetic, lipid-lowering, and broad-spectrum antibacterial effects, its planar cationic structure allows it to efficiently intercalate DNA and disrupt microbial replication, consequently, this compound holds significant therapeutic potential in the management of metabolic syndrome (diabetes, hyperlipidemia) and infectious diseases, often studied as an AMPK activator to regulate energy metabolism.

RNA information: TCT8-4 has two conserved regions (5'-AUACCA-3' and 5'-CCUUGG(C/A)AG-3') . It binds theophylline  in a Mg-dependent way, showing 10,900-fold higher affinity for theophylline than caffeine. It aids in diagnosing diseases like asthma/bronchitis, where theophylline is used as a bronchodilator, by distinguishing theophylline from similar compounds.
Small_molecule_information: 1-methyl-3,7-dihydropurine-2,6-dione (also referred to as 1-methylxanthine) is a theophylline derivative that lacks the N3 methyl group of theophylline; it is a monomethylxanthine in which a methyl group is attached to N-1 and the imidazole ring is protonated at N-7, occurs naturally as a minor purine alkaloid and has been identified as a mouse metabolite. It has no mentioned specific biological function; its key relevance lies in binding theophylline-binding RNA. Compared to theophylline, the absence of the N-3 methyl and the presence of only a single N-1 methyl reduce its complementarity to the TCT8-4 RNA binding pocket, making it unable to fit into the hydrophobic pocket formed by RNAs C8, A28, and U24, though it still relies on core RNA bases (C22, U24) for weak binding, which explains its lower binding affinity, and both the N-3 methyl and the N-7 hydrogen-bond donor are critical for high-affinity recognition by the evolved RNA aptamer.

RNA information: r(CUG)₆ is a short ribonucleic acid fragment consisting of six consecutive CUG repeat units. Structurally, it can form a more stable hairpin secondary structure than short-chain r(CUG)₂/r(CUG)₄, with multiple characteristic U·U mismatches in the stem region and six 5′CUG/3′GUC internal loops (a key feature simulating the structure of long-chain pathogenic CUG repeat sequences). In some cases, its conformational stability can be further enhanced by GC base pairs (similar to the GC clamp design of (CUG)₁₂ mentioned in the document). Functionally, it does not possess the toxicity of long expanded r(CUG)ⁿ ,which forms nuclear foci and sequesters MBNL1 (Muscleblind-like family protein) — but its specific binding affinity to MBNL1 is significantly improved. Competitive dialysis experiments showed that its binding preference for CUG repeats is increased by more than 4-fold compared with MBNL1 protein, and it exhibits a binding stoichiometry of approximately 1:2 (drug:5′CUG/3′GUC internal loop) when binding to small-molecule compounds (such as lomofungin and dilomofungin). Its core role is as an in vitro research tool to analyze the binding mechanism between MBNL1 and CUG repeat sequences, verify the inhibitory effect of small molecules on their interaction, and provide a structural and functional model closer to the characteristics of long-chain CUG repeat sequences for the study of the pathogenic mechanism of myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1) and the preliminary screening of therapeutic drugs
Small_molecule_information: methyl 7-hydroxy-6-(hydroxymethylidene)-4,9-dioxo-10H-phenazine-1-carboxylate, also referred to as lomofungin, is a derivative of phenazine, a core structure found in natural pigments with anthraquinone-like features, substituted with hydroxyl, keto, and methyl carboxylate groups; it is a natural antimicrobial agent produced by Streptomyces lomondensis that undergoes spontaneous dimerization in DMSO to form dilomofungin, and in real activity, phenazine compounds are well-known for their redox activity, enabling them to generate reactive oxygen species, this compound has potential antimicrobial activity as certain phenazine natural products are potent antibiotics, while it has also been identified as a potent inhibitor of MBNL1-CUG RNA binding, binds selectively to RNA hairpins containing CUG repeats particularly those with UU mismatches and disrupts the interaction between MBNL1 protein and pathogenic CUGexp RNA, in cellular models of myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1) it partially restores MBNL1-dependent alternative splicing and increases the diffuse nucleoplasmic localization of MBNL1 indicating functional reversal of RNA toxicity, though its dimeric form dilomofungin, while more potent in vitro, unexpectedly stabilizes CUGexp RNA and leads to its accumulation in nuclear foci highlighting a potential limitation in the therapeutic use of high-affinity RNA ligands, and these findings suggest that it represents a promising scaffold for developing small-molecule therapies against DM1 provided that off-target effects on RNA turnover are carefully managed.
